---
title: "Final Model"
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(leaps)
library(sjPlot)
library(lmSubsets)
library(tidyr)

data <- read.delim("housing-prices-ge19.txt", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
data = data |> 
  filter(Bathrooms != 0) |>
  mutate(Price = log(Price))
train_data <- data %>% filter(Test == 0)
test_data <- data %>% filter(Test == 1)
```

```{r}

str(data)

# Identify categorical variables (character or logical)
cat_vars <- names(data)[sapply(data, is.character) | sapply(data, is.logical)]
cat_vars


data[cat_vars] <- lapply(data[cat_vars], factor)

# Check structure again
str(data)

# View how factors are encoded (dummy variables)
model.matrix(~ Heat.Type, data = data)[1:5, ]

```

```{r}

outlier_indices <- c(84, 641, 763)

data_cleaned <- train_data %>%
  slice(-outlier_indices)

# Display the change
cat("Original number of rows in 'data':", nrow(data), "\n")
cat("Number of rows after removing outliers:", nrow(data_cleaned), "\n")

# Now, use 'data_cleaned' for refitting your linear model to see 
# the improvement in assumptions.
```

## Full and Null Model

```{r}
M0 = lm(Price ~ 1,data=train_data) # Null model
M1 = lm(Price ~ .,data=train_data) # Full model
round(summary(M1)$coef, 3)
```

```{r}
res = bind_rows(broom::glance(M1), 
                broom::glance(M0))
res$model= c("M1","M0")
res |> pivot_longer(
  cols = -model, 
  names_to = "metric", 
  values_to = "value") |> 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = "model") |> 
  gt::gt() |> 
  gt::fmt_number(columns = 2:3, 
                 decimals = 2) |> 
  gt::sub_missing()
```

Backward Stepwise Using AIC

```{r}
step.back.aic = step(M1, 
                     direction = "backward", 
                     trace = FALSE)
round(summary(step.back.aic)$coef,3)
```

```{r}
step.back.aic |>
  broom::glance() |> 
  round(2) |> t()
```

## Forward Search Using AIC

```{r}
step.fwd.aic = step(M0, scope = list(lower = M0, upper = M1),
                    direction = "forward", trace = FALSE)
summary(step.fwd.aic)
```

## **Comparing forward and backwards stepwise**

```{r}
sjPlot::tab_model(step.fwd.aic, step.back.aic, show.ci = FALSE, show.aic = TRUE,
                  dv.labels = c("Forward model", "Backward model"))
```

## Exhaustive Search

```{r}
exh_leaps = regsubsets(Price ~., data = train_data, nvmax = 15)
summary(exh_leaps)$outmat
```

```{r}
exh = lmSubsets(Price ~ ., data = train_data, nbest = 15)
plot(exh)
```

```{r}
library(ggfortify)
autoplot(step.back.aic, which = 1:2)
```

## Final Model

```{r}
final_model = lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
    Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size, 
    data = train_data)
autoplot(final_model, which = 1:2)
summary(final_model)
```

```{r}
sjPlot::tab_model(
  final_model, 
  show.ci = FALSE,
  dv.labels = "Log Price")
```

```{r}
library(caret)
cv_final = train(
  Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
    Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size, 
  data = train_data,
  method = "lm",
  trControl = trainControl(
    method = "cv", number = 10,
    verboseIter = FALSE
  )
)
cv_final
```

## Model Stability

```{r}
library(mplot)
vis.art = vis(M1)
plot(vis.art, which="vip", interactive = TRUE)
plot(vis.art, which = "boot", interactive = TRUE)
```

## Outliers

```{r}
# View specific outlier points
outliers <- train_data[c(84, 641, 763), ]

# Print them
print(outliers)

# Or view nicely in a table
View(outliers)

```

```{r}
# Fit your regression model (assuming it's called model)
model <- lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
    Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size, 
    data = train_data)

# Plot residuals vs fitted values
plot(model$fitted.values, rstandard(model),
     main = "Residuals vs Fitted",
     xlab = "Fitted values",
     ylab = "Standardized Residuals")

# Highlight the outliers
points(model$fitted.values[c(84, 641, 763)],
       rstandard(model)[c(84, 641, 763)],
       col = "red", pch = 19)

# Add labels
text(model$fitted.values[c(84, 641, 763)],
     rstandard(model)[c(84, 641, 763)],
     labels = c(84, 641, 763), pos = 4, col = "red")

```

## Appendix: Weighted Least Squares Regression Model

```{r}
final_model <- lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
    Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size + Pct.College, 
    data = data_cleaned)
# Get residuals and fitted values
res <- residuals(final_model)
fitted_vals <- fitted(final_model)

# Regress squared residuals on fitted values to model heteroskedasticity
var_model <- lm(I(res^2) ~ fitted_vals)
summary(var_model)
weights <- 1 / fitted(var_model)
wls_model <- lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
                  Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size + Pct.College, 
                data = data_cleaned, 
                weights = weights)

summary(wls_model)
autoplot(wls_model, which = 1:2)
```

```{r}
# --- Step 1: Initial OLS Model ---
final_model <- lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
    Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size + Pct.College, 
    data = data_cleaned)

# Get residuals and fitted values
res <- residuals(final_model)
fitted_vals <- fitted(final_model)

# --- Step 2: Model the Variance using log transformation (FIX) ---
# We model the log of the squared residuals on the fitted values.
# This stabilizes the variance model and is generally more robust.
var_model <- lm(log(res^2) ~ fitted_vals) 

# --- Step 3: Calculate Weights (FIX) ---
# The weights are the inverse of the estimated variance (which is exp(fitted_vals))
weights <- 1 / exp(fitted(var_model))

# --- Step 4: Final Weighted Least Squares (WLS) Regression ---
wls_model <- lm(Price ~ Living.Area + Land.Value + Bathrooms + Waterfront + 
                  Age + New.Construct + Heat.Type + Lot.Size + Pct.College, 
                data = data_cleaned, 
                weights = weights)

# --- Step 5: Output and Diagnostics ---
summary(wls_model)
autoplot(wls_model, which = 1:2)
```
